title: CRUD: The Four Operations
intro: A developer is writing SQL to update a user's email address, but their query changes EVERY user's email instead of just one. It's like using an eraser in a notebook without checking which line you're on -- you might erase someone else's notes. This is a classic (and terrifying) mistake with the UPDATE command.

teach.title: CRUD = The Four Things You Do With Data
teach.body: Think of a contacts list on your phone. You can **Create** a new contact, **Read** existing contacts, **Update** someone's phone number, or **Delete** a contact you don't need. Every app does these same four things. In the database world, we call them **CRUD** -- Create, Read, Update, Delete.

teach.title: The Notebook With an Eraser
teach.body: CRUD is like using a notebook with a pencil and eraser. You **write** new entries (Create), **read** what you wrote earlier (Read), **erase and rewrite** a line to fix a typo (Update), or **tear out a page** you no longer need (Delete). But imagine erasing without looking at which line you are on -- you might erase someone else's notes. That is exactly what happens when you forget the WHERE clause in SQL.

teach.title: The Four SQL Commands
teach.body:
Each CRUD operation has its own SQL command:

1. **Create** = `INSERT INTO users (name, email) VALUES ('Alice', 'alice@mail.com')`
2. **Read** = `SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = 1`
3. **Update** = `UPDATE users SET email = 'new@mail.com' WHERE id = 1`
4. **Delete** = `DELETE FROM users WHERE id = 1`

The most dangerous operations are UPDATE and DELETE -- forgetting the WHERE clause affects EVERY row!

teach.title: The Danger of Forgetting WHERE
teach.annotation: Without WHERE, the UPDATE applies to ALL rows. This is the #1 most common database disaster. Always double-check your WHERE clause before running UPDATE or DELETE.

teach.body: CRUD is the heartbeat of every money-making app. Signing up a user is Create, showing a product page is Read, changing a subscription plan is Update, and canceling an account is Delete. Master CRUD and you can vibecode any app that handles real customers and real transactions.
CRUD stands for Create (INSERT), Read (SELECT), Update (UPDATE), and Delete (DELETE). Always include a WHERE clause with UPDATE and DELETE, or you'll affect every row in the table.

code.text: -- Actual: ALL 10,000 users now have the same email!

exercise.prompt: The developer wanted to update only user #42's email, but instead every user in the database now has 'alice.new@example.com' as their email address. 10,000 users affected! Find the line with the missing piece.
exercise.explanation: The UPDATE command without a WHERE clause updates EVERY row in the table. Adding 'WHERE id = 42' tells the database 'only change the row where the id column equals 42.' This is one of the most important safety rules in SQL: never run UPDATE or DELETE without a WHERE clause. Many developers first run a SELECT with the same WHERE clause to verify it matches the right rows before running the UPDATE.
